# LAB: R Projects and R Markdown {#lab16}

Welcome to the R Projects and R Markdown Lab! In this lab, you will build a complete, reproducible data analysis workflow from scratch using R Projects and R Markdown. Rather than just reading about these tools, you'll set up your own project, write markdown, embed code, and create interactive outputs. By the end, you'll have a polished, self-contained analysis document that you can share with collaborators or submit as coursework. This lab takes heavy inspiration from [Zachary M. Smith](https://github.com/zsmith27/rmarkdown_crash-course).

## Learning goals {.unnumbered}

- Set up an R Project and understand how it organizes your workspace and simplifies file paths.

- Create and knit an R Markdown document using multiple output formats (HTML, PDF, Word).

- Write effective markdown syntax including headings, lists, bold/italic text, hyperlinks, and images.

- Customize YAML headers to control document appearance (themes, table of contents, code folding).

- Use code chunks and inline code to integrate R output into your narrative.

- Build interactive outputs using packages like DT, plotly, and leaflet.


## Getting started and warming up {.unnumbered}

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
options(
  htmltools.dir.version = FALSE, # for blogdown
  show.signif.stars = FALSE, # for regression output
  digits = 2
)
```

### Packages {.unnumbered}

In this lab, we will work with the **tidyverse**, **DT**, **plotly**, and **leaflet** packages. We can install and load them with the following code:

```{r load-packages, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
if (!require("tidyverse")) install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)

if (!require("DT")) install.packages("DT")
library(DT)

if (!require("plotly")) install.packages("plotly")
library(plotly)

if (!require("leaflet")) install.packages("leaflet")
library(leaflet)
```

## Exercises {.unnumbered}

### Part 1: Setting Up Your R Project {.unnumbered}

R Projects establish a self-contained environment for any data analysis project. They help in organizing your work and ensuring that file paths are consistent, making your work portable and easier to share with collaborators.


## Exercise 1: Create an R Project {.unnumbered}

In this exercise, you will create a new R Project from scratch. This project will serve as the home for all the work you do in this lab.

**1.1.** Create a new R Project in RStudio:

1. Click on the drop-down menu in the top right of your RStudio window and select "New Project."
2. Select "New Directory" within the "Create Project" window.
3. Select "New Project" within the "Project Type" window.
4. Enter a project name (e.g., "rmarkdown_lab") and choose a directory, then click "Create Project."

> **Tip**: Create a "projects" folder that will act as your parent directory for all R Projects. This will make it much easier to navigate to and between projects.

![](img/r_project/fresh_project.png)

Once your project is open, notice how RStudio updates to reflect the project context. You can confirm which project is active by looking at the top-right corner of RStudio.

**1.2.** Inside your new project, create the following folder structure:

- A `data` folder for datasets
- An `output` folder for rendered documents

You can create folders using the "New Folder" button in the Files pane, or by running:

```{r exercise-1-2, eval=FALSE}
dir.create("data")
dir.create("output")
```

**1.3.** Navigate between projects by clicking the drop-down menu at the top right of RStudio and selecting a recent project. Open your new project again to confirm you're in the right workspace. What do you notice about the working directory when you switch projects?

![](img/r_project/nav_project.png){width="400px"}


🧶 ✅ ⬆️ Knit, commit, and push your changes to GitHub with an appropriate commit message. Make sure to commit and push all changed files so that your Git pane is cleared up afterwards.

### Part 2: Creating and Knitting an R Markdown Document {.unnumbered}

R Markdown documents let you combine narrative text, code, and output in a single reproducible document. In this section, you'll create your first `.Rmd` file and learn how to compile (knit) it.

## Exercise 2: Your First R Markdown Document {.unnumbered}

**2.1.** Create a new R Markdown document in RStudio:

1. Click the new document button (the white page with a green plus).
2. Select "R Markdown."
3. Provide a title (e.g., "My First Analysis"), enter your name as author, select HTML as the default output format, and click "OK."

![](img/rmarkdown/rmarkdown_button.png){width="400px"}

A new R Markdown document will appear with some default instructions and example text/code.

**2.2.** Knit the default document by clicking the **Knit** button (the ball of yarn icon) in the toolbar. If prompted, install any required packages.

![](img/rmarkdown/knit_button.png){width="160px"}

Examine the rendered HTML output. What elements do you see in the output that correspond to parts of the `.Rmd` source file?

**2.3.** Using the drop-down arrow next to the Knit button, try knitting to a different format (e.g., Word). How does the output differ from the HTML version?

> Note: Knitting to PDF requires LaTeX. If you don't have LaTeX installed, you can install TinyTeX in R with `tinytex::install_tinytex()`.

<details>
  <summary>Click for a hint about output formats</summary>
  The available output formats include:

  - `html_document` — the default, supports interactive features
  - `pdf_document` — requires LaTeX, no interactive features
  - `word_document` — requires Microsoft Word

  Changing the output format updates your YAML header automatically.
</details>

🧶 ✅ ⬆️ Knit, commit, and push your changes to GitHub with an appropriate commit message. Make sure to commit and push all changed files so that your Git pane is cleared up afterwards.

### Part 3: Markdown Syntax Essentials {.unnumbered}

Now that you can create and knit documents, let's learn the markdown syntax that controls formatting.

## Exercise 3: Writing Markdown {.unnumbered}

Delete everything below the YAML header in your `.Rmd` file. You're going to build your document from scratch.

**3.1.** Add the following level-1 headers and knit the document to see the structure:

- Introduction
- Workflow
- Import Data
- Analysis
- Conclusions

**3.2.** Below the "Introduction" header, write a paragraph that includes:

- At least one **bold** word (surround with `**`)
- At least one *italicized* word (surround with `*`)
- A hyperlink to the [R Markdown: The Definitive Guide](https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/)

<details>
  <summary>Click for a hint about hyperlinks</summary>
  To create a hyperlink with display text, use the syntax: `[display text](URL)`

  For example: `[R Markdown Guide](https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/)`
</details>

**3.3.** Below the "Workflow" header, create an ordered list describing the workflow we've used so far:

1. Create an R Project
2. Add folders for organization
3. Create an R Markdown document
4. Knit the document to render output

Add at least two sub-bullets with helpful details under any of the items above.

<details>
  <summary>Click for a hint about lists</summary>
  Ordered lists use numbers followed by periods. Sub-bullets use four spaces of indentation followed by a dash (`-`):

  ```
  1. First item
      - Sub-bullet detail
      - Another detail
  2. Second item
  ```
</details>

**3.4.** Add an image to your document. You can use any image file in your project, or try the markdown syntax:

```
![Caption text here](path/to/image.png){width="200px"}
```

You can center an image using HTML:
```
<center>
![Caption](path/to/image.png){width="200px"}
</center>
```

🧶 ✅ ⬆️ Knit, commit, and push your changes to GitHub with an appropriate commit message. Make sure to commit and push all changed files so that your Git pane is cleared up afterwards.

### Part 4: YAML Headers {.unnumbered}

The YAML header (enclosed by `---` at the top of your document) controls the document's metadata and formatting. YAML stands for "YAML Ain't Markup Language."

<center>
![](img/rmarkdown/yaml_1.png)
</center>

## Exercise 4: Customizing Your YAML Header {.unnumbered}

**4.1.** Edit your YAML header to include:

- A meaningful title
- Your name as author
- A dynamic date that updates each time you knit (hint: use inline R code)

<details>
  <summary>Click for a hint about dynamic dates</summary>
  You can use inline R code in the YAML header for a dynamic date. The syntax looks like:

  `date: "Last compiled on 2025-01-15"`

  But replace the static date with inline R code that calls `Sys.Date()`.
</details>

**4.2.** Add a table of contents to your document by modifying the `output` section of your YAML:

```yaml
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
```

Knit the document. How does the floating table of contents change navigation?

**4.3.** Change the document theme by adding `theme: journal` to your YAML output options. Knit and compare the appearance.

<details>
  <summary>Click for a hint about available themes</summary>
  Some available themes include: `default`, `cerulean`, `journal`, `flatly`, `darkly`, `readable`, `spacelab`, `united`, `cosmo`, `lumen`, `paper`, `sandstone`, `simplex`, `yeti`.

  You can browse examples at: https://www.datadreaming.org/post/r-markdown-theme-gallery/
</details>

**4.4.** Add code folding to your document by adding `code_folding: hide` to your YAML output options. Knit the document and observe how code chunks are now hidden by default with a "Code" button to reveal them.

<center>
![](img/rmarkdown/folding_hide.png)
</center>


🧶 ✅ ⬆️ Knit, commit, and push your changes to GitHub with an appropriate commit message. Make sure to commit and push all changed files so that your Git pane is cleared up afterwards.


### Part 5: Code Chunks and Inline Code {.unnumbered}

Up to this point, we've mostly written a standard markdown document. Now it's time to integrate R code to create reproducible figures, tables, and text.

## Exercise 5: Working with Code Chunks {.unnumbered}

To insert a code chunk, press **Ctrl + Alt + I** (Windows/Linux) or **Cmd + Option + I** (Mac). A code chunk will appear where you can write and execute R code.

**Shortcuts you'll want to learn:**

- `Ctrl + Alt + I` — Insert a new code chunk
- `Ctrl + Enter` — Run the current line of code
- `Ctrl + Alt + R` — Run all code in the document
- `Ctrl + Shift + F10` — Restart R

**5.1.** Add a code chunk that loads the `iris` dataset and prints a summary. Give your code chunk a meaningful name in the header (e.g., `{r iris-summary}`).

```{r exercise-5-1-example, eval=FALSE}
data(iris)
summary(iris)
```

**5.2.** Add a code chunk that creates a scatter plot of the `iris` data. Experiment with the following chunk options:

- `echo=FALSE` — hide the code, show only the plot
- `fig.width=8, fig.height=5` — control figure dimensions
- `fig.cap="Scatter plot of iris petal dimensions"` — add a caption

```{r exercise-5-2-example, eval=FALSE}
ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Iris Petal Dimensions",
    x = "Petal Width",
    y = "Petal Length"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**5.3.** Use inline code to dynamically report a statistic in your narrative text. For example, write a sentence like:

> "The mean sepal length in the iris dataset is `r mean(iris$Sepal.Length)`."

The inline code syntax is: `` `r
knitr::inline_expr("mean(iris$Sepal.Length)")` ``

**5.4.** Add a global options chunk just below your YAML header that sets default figure dimensions for the entire document:

```{r exercise-5-4-example, eval=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4)
```

Set the chunk header to `include=FALSE` so this setup chunk doesn't appear in your rendered document.

<details>
  <summary>Click for a hint about chunk options</summary>
  Common chunk options include:

  - `echo` — show/hide the code (TRUE/FALSE)
  - `eval` — run/skip the code (TRUE/FALSE)
  - `include` — include chunk in output (TRUE/FALSE)
  - `message` and `warning` — show/hide messages and warnings
  - `fig.width`, `fig.height` — figure dimensions in inches
  - `fig.cap` — figure caption

  A comprehensive list is at: https://yihui.org/knitr/options/
</details>

🧶 ✅ ⬆️ Knit, commit, and push your changes to GitHub with an appropriate commit message. Make sure to commit and push all changed files so that your Git pane is cleared up afterwards.


### Part 6: Interactive Features {.unnumbered}

Interactive tables and figures are a great way to make your HTML documents more engaging and useful for data exploration.

## Exercise 6: Adding Interactivity {.unnumbered}

**6.1.** Create an interactive data table using the **DT** package. Add this code chunk to the "Import Data" section of your document:

```{r lab_rmarkdown-code-dt-package}
library(DT)

datatable(iris)
```

Explore the interactive table — try sorting columns, searching, and filtering.

**6.2.** Convert your scatter plot from Exercise 5.2 into an interactive plot using **plotly**. The `ggplotly()` function makes this easy:

```{r plotly_example, message=FALSE}
library(plotly)

p_scatter <- ggplot(iris, aes(
  x = Petal.Width, y = Petal.Length,
  color = Species
)) +
  geom_point()

ggplotly(p_scatter)
```

Hover over points, zoom in, and try the toolbar buttons at the top right of the plot. What information appears when you hover?

**6.3.** Create an interactive map using **leaflet**. This example maps earthquake data that comes built into R:

```{r lab_rmarkdown-code-leaflet-basic}
library(leaflet)

data("quakes")

leaflet(
  data = quakes,
  options = leafletOptions(
    minZoom = 4,
    maxZoom = 18
  )
) %>%
  addTiles() %>%
  addCircleMarkers(~long, ~lat,
    fillOpacity = 0.75,
    stroke = FALSE,
    popup = paste(
      "Magnitude:", quakes$mag, "<br/>",
      "Latitude:", quakes$lat, "<br/>",
      "Longitude:", quakes$long
    )
  )
```

**6.4.** Modify the leaflet map to cluster nearby points for better visualization of density. Add `clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions()` to your `addCircleMarkers()` call. What changes when you zoom in and out?

```{r lab_rmarkdown-code-leaflet-cluster}
library(leaflet)

data("quakes")

leaflet(
  data = quakes,
  options = leafletOptions(
    minZoom = 4,
    maxZoom = 18
  )
) %>%
  addTiles() %>%
  addCircleMarkers(~long, ~lat,
    fillOpacity = 0.75,
    stroke = FALSE,
    popup = paste(
      "Magnitude:", quakes$mag, "<br/>",
      "Latitude:", quakes$lat, "<br/>",
      "Longitude:", quakes$long
    ),
    clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions()
  )
```

🧶 ✅ ⬆️ Knit, commit, and push your changes to GitHub with an appropriate commit message. Make sure to commit and push all changed files so that your Git pane is cleared up afterwards.


## Stretch Tasks (Optional) {.unnumbered}

If you finish early, try these challenges to deepen your R Markdown skills.

**7.1.** **Child documents**: Break your R Markdown document into multiple "child" documents, one for each major section. In your parent document, source each child using `child = 'sections/filename.Rmd'` in the code chunk header. This strategy keeps large documents manageable.

![](img/rmarkdown/child_document.png)

**7.2.** **R Notebook**: Change your output to `html_notebook` and explore the difference. With an R Notebook, you can preview output without re-knitting the entire document. Try running individual chunks and watching the preview update in the Viewer pane.

**7.3.** **Custom Word template**: If you have Microsoft Word installed, create a custom Word template with your preferred formatting (fonts, colors, spacing). Reference it in your YAML header:

```yaml
output:
  word_document:
    reference_docx: my_template.docx
```

**7.4.** **Presentations**: Try creating a presentation using R Markdown. Change your output to `ioslides_presentation` or `slidy_presentation` and experiment with creating slides using markdown headers.


## Conclusion {.unnumbered}

Congratulations on completing the R Projects and R Markdown Lab! You've built a complete, reproducible analysis document from scratch, learning how to organize your workspace with R Projects, write formatted text with markdown, customize your output with YAML headers, integrate code and visualizations with code chunks, and add interactivity with DT, plotly, and leaflet.

__Final Checklist__

- Ensure you have created and knit your R Markdown document without errors.
- Review your rendered output and confirm that all sections, code chunks, and interactive features are working correctly.
- Check that your project folder is well-organized with appropriate subfolders.

__Reflect on Your Learning__

- How does using R Projects change the way you organize your analysis work?
- What advantages does R Markdown offer over writing code in plain R scripts?
- Which interactive feature (DT, plotly, or leaflet) do you think will be most useful in your own work?

### Next Steps {.unnumbered}

- Commit all your changes to your project repository. Use a comprehensive commit message that reflects the completion of this lab.
- Push your changes to ensure everything is updated in your remote repository.

🧶 ✅ ⬆️ Knit, commit, and push your changes to GitHub with an appropriate commit message. Make sure to commit and push all changed files so that your Git pane is cleared up afterwards and review the md document on GitHub to make sure you're happy with the final state of your work.
